food_name: Ikan Keli Berlado (Catfish in Chili Sambal)
food_subgroup: Deep-fried fish with sambal — Malay / Minangkabau preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic catfish food product entry; no specific entry exists for berlado-style deep-fried catfish with chili sambal. 'Exact' used for the general category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for deep-fried catfish preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Ikan Keli Berlado. A00GJ (cooked fish) is the closest defensible anchor. 'Exact' used for the general category. No code invented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific traditional dish preparation. Null is appropriate.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 200 g whole fish with sambal)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ikan Keli Berlado is one whole small catfish with berlado sambal, weighing approximately 200 g, commonly served alongside steamed rice in a Nasi Padang or Nasi Campur meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Fried Fish and Sambal Dishes; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central catfish nutrient profile (NDB 15011) for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ikan Keli Berlado as a composite dish. Macronutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried fish and sambal dish entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central catfish (channel catfish, cooked, breaded and fried, NDB 15011) for protein, fat, and energy. Micronutrients are cross-referenced with USDA catfish and chili pepper entries. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate for the whole dish per 100 g edible portion. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.5 g per 100 g based on deep-frying in palm or vegetable oil, consistent with HPB fried fish benchmarks.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate fried fish dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ikan Keli Berlado. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate fried fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (4.5 g per 100 g), primarily from the chili sambal. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 9.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.15, rounded to 3.2. This is consistent with low-GL classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; approximately 760 mg per 200 g serving) from added salt in marinade and sambal, combined with deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g, request reduced salt in the sambal preparation, avoid reusing frying oil, and pair with unsalted steamed vegetables rather than additional salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (16.5 g per 100 g) support glycemic control; estimated GI of 35 is in the low range
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ikan Keli Berlado is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 200 g serving without added sugar in the sambal, but total meal glycemic load should account for any accompanying rice or starchy sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat intake; high sodium is an additional cardiovascular risk factor, though catfish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings, prefer grilled or steamed catfish preparations where possible, and monitor total saturated fat and sodium intake across the full meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying may promote pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); nightshade vegetables (chilies) may be a trigger for some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this dish in moderation and monitor personal tolerance to nightshade-containing preparations; grilled catfish with minimal chili is a lower-inflammation alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Keli Berlado is a traditional Malay and Minangkabau dish consisting of whole catfish (ikan keli) that is first marinated in turmeric and salt, then deep-fried until the skin is exceptionally crispy while the flesh remains moist and tender. The fried fish is then generously coated with berlado sambal — a coarsely pounded or chopped mixture of red chilies, bird's eye chilies, shallots, and garlic that is sautéed in oil until fragrant and slightly caramelized. Unlike smooth blended sambal pastes, berlado retains a chunky, rustic texture that delivers intense heat and bold flavor. The dish is a staple of Nasi Padang and Nasi Campur stalls across Malaysia and Indonesia, where it is served alongside steamed rice and other side dishes. Nutritionally, Ikan Keli Berlado provides approximately 215 kcal per 100 g, with 16.5 g protein, 14.8 g fat, and only 4.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option with a low estimated glycemic index of 35. The dish is a good source of niacin (5.2 mg), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg), selenium (28 mcg), and phosphorus (180 mg). Sodium content is significant at 380 mg per 100 g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. It is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when rice portions are controlled. The Indonesian equivalent is known as Lele Balado, reflecting the Minangkabau culinary tradition shared across the Malay Archipelago.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Ikan Keli Berlado as a composite dish. Macronutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried fish and sambal dish benchmarks and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central catfish entries. Saturated fat is estimated from deep-frying oil type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate fried fish dishes. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g per 100 g) is estimated based on deep-frying in palm or vegetable oil, consistent with HPB Singapore fried fish benchmarks; no direct measurement available for this specific dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this traditional composite dish are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Ikan Keli Berlado exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (35 × 9.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.2 g. 5. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE estimated from catfish and red chili contributions; original input reported 65 mcg which is retained as a reasonable estimate. 6. This dish is a Malay/Minangkabau preparation; Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and are set to null. 7. The dish is classified as halal and gluten-free under standard preparation conditions without cross-contamination.
